title: How Measurement is Evolving in Next-Best Action
gist: BCG argues that most next-best-action (NBA) marketing programs measure engagement rather than causal incrementality, and that rigorous incrementality testing typically reveals 20%-40% of active programs deliver marginal to negative lift. It maps a three-level maturity path from campaign metrics to agentic measurement, introducing Big M / Little m holdout frameworks and cataloging the governance pitfalls that undermine credible measurement.

themes.theme: Incrementality vs. activation as the core measurement gap

insights.claim: When organizations deploy rigorous incrementality testing, 20% to 40% of their active next-best-action programs typically deliver marginal to negative lift, redirecting demand rather than creating it.
insights.speaker: Silvio Palumbo
insights.so_what: A large share of marketing spend on personalization/NBA programs may be harvesting demand that would have converted anyway; investors and operators should treat unmeasured NBA ROI claims skeptically and probe for holdout-based proof.
insights.quote: they typically find that 20% to 40% of their active programs deliver marginal to negative lift
insights.locator: Our research and direct experience indicate

insights.claim: Simplistic marketing measurement that focuses on activation metrics like offers delivered, open rates, and win rates cannot distinguish actions that positively influenced behavior from those that were neutral or cannibalistic.
insights.speaker: Silvio Palumbo
insights.so_what: Dashboards showing high engagement can mask value destruction; the signal to watch is whether a vendor or program can prove causal lift, not response rates.
insights.locator: Proving value realization is the most underestimated challenge

insights.claim: Last-touch attribution compounds measurement error by assigning full credit to the action that preceded conversion, conflating selection effects with treatment effects because the customers chosen for an offer were already the most likely to convert.
insights.speaker: Silvio Palumbo
insights.so_what: Attribution-based ROI figures systematically overstate program impact; any measurement claim resting on last-touch attribution should be discounted.
insights.quote: This conflates selection effects with treatment effects.
insights.locator: In fact, last-touch attribution compounds the problem

insights.claim: Measurement maturity progresses through three levels: Level 1 campaign metrics (engagement, not causation), Level 2 incrementality testing with randomized holdouts, and Level 3 agentic measurement where agents interpret evidence and execute optimization.
insights.speaker: Silvio Palumbo
insights.so_what: Provides a diagnostic ladder for assessing how advanced a company's marketing analytics really are; most organizations sit at Level 1 despite Level 2 being needed for causal claims.
insights.locator: Three Levels of Measurement Maturity

insights.claim: The main barrier to reaching Level 2 incrementality testing is organizational, not technical, because randomized holdout groups require deliberately not marketing to some customers, creating tension with revenue-focused business teams.
insights.speaker: Silvio Palumbo
insights.so_what: The bottleneck to better measurement is incentive alignment and executive will, so leadership commitment is a better tell of capability than tooling.
insights.quote: an unavoidable opportunity cost of measurement
insights.locator: The challenge of getting to Level 2 is often organizational

insights.claim: At Level 3 agentic measurement, an agent reads causal evidence and runs read-and-react cycles at a speed marketers cannot match, shifting the human role from interpreting and deciding to reviewing and governing.
insights.speaker: Silvio Palumbo
insights.so_what: Signals a structural shift in marketing headcount toward governance roles and away from manual analysis, favoring vendors building agent-native optimization loops.
insights.locator: Level 3: Agentic Measurement

themes.theme: Big M / Little m holdout framework

insights.claim: Big M measurement uses a global holdout of customers receiving no agent-composed actions to validate whether the entire NBA system creates value, providing the accuracy needed to earn a CFO's signoff.
insights.speaker: Silvio Palumbo
insights.so_what: The presence of a maintained global holdout is a concrete, checkable indicator that a program's financials are credibly validated rather than asserted.
insights.quote: Big M validates the system’s financials to a degree of accuracy necessary to earn a CFO’s signoff.
insights.locator: Big M measurement addresses the program-level question

insights.claim: Big M global-holdout measurement often overstates program-level impact because of the halo effect, the pull-forward effect, experiment contamination, and operational errors.
insights.speaker: Silvio Palumbo
insights.so_what: Even rigorous program-level holdouts can inflate ROI, so headline incrementality numbers should be stress-tested for these specific biases.
insights.locator: Unfortunately, Big M often overstates program-level impact

insights.claim: Little m measurement sets up statistically significant local holdouts within the system, splitting the test audience into randomly assigned subgroups to determine whether a specific action creates incremental value versus the next best alternative.
insights.speaker: Silvio Palumbo
insights.so_what: Enables granular, contamination-free optimization of which offer or channel drives lift for a specific segment, the operational engine of a measurement-led program.
insights.locator: Little m measurement addresses the tactic-level question

insights.claim: Measurement decisions span three tiers—strategic (where to invest), tactical (what to invest in), and operational (how to optimize execution)—but Level 1 organizations are limited almost entirely to the operational tier.
insights.speaker: Silvio Palumbo
insights.so_what: Companies stuck on click-through and A/B creative tests cannot answer budget-allocation questions with causal evidence, a gap that limits confident capital reallocation.
insights.locator: Answer Questions at Every Tier

insights.claim: A complete measurement toolkit combines four complementary approaches—in-platform metrics, modeling, customer insights, and experimentation—each answering a different question on a different time horizon.
insights.speaker: Silvio Palumbo
insights.so_what: Organizations that lean heavily on in-platform metrics alone are under-measuring; triangulating all four is the standard to look for in a mature analytics function.
insights.locator: Breaking Down the Measurement Toolkit

insights.claim: Doubly robust learners, which combine outcome modeling with propensity weighting, are especially valuable for estimating incremental effects because they provide consistent estimates even when one of the two components is misspecified.
insights.speaker: Silvio Palumbo
insights.so_what: Names a specific technical method that signals analytics sophistication; its use distinguishes rigorous causal modeling from naive uplift estimation.
insights.locator: Among the most rigorous approaches for estimating incremental effects

insights.claim: Customer insights such as brand tracking, satisfaction surveys, and cohort analyses capture long-term effects that short-term metrics miss, revealing whether personalization is increasing customer lifetime value or merely pulling purchases forward.
insights.speaker: Silvio Palumbo
insights.so_what: Short-horizon lift can conceal demand cannibalization and brand erosion, so long-term LTV and brand-equity signals should accompany any incrementality claim.
insights.quote: Is personalization increasing customer lifetime value or just pulling purchases forward?
insights.locator: Customer Insights

insights.claim: Holdout contamination biases measurement toward underestimating program impact when holdout customers receive uncontrolled touchpoints such as branch staff calls, retail promotions, or parallel paid media.
insights.speaker: Silvio Palumbo
insights.so_what: Cross-channel discipline over who may contact holdout customers is a prerequisite for trustworthy results; without it, measured impact is distorted.
insights.locator: Holdout Contamination

insights.claim: Novelty effects inflate early program results, so effective measurement requires letting programs run past the novelty period—typically 8 to 12 weeks depending on purchase cycle—before drawing conclusions.
insights.speaker: Silvio Palumbo
insights.so_what: Launch-window results should be discounted; a specific 8-12 week seasoning threshold gives a concrete bar for when to trust program metrics.
insights.locator: Novelty Effects

insights.claim: The most underestimated measurement challenge is political: when incrementality testing reveals a flagship program delivers limited lift, program owners are incentivized to attack the methodology, the most common backlash after first-time incrementality testing.
insights.speaker: Silvio Palumbo
insights.so_what: Organizational resistance, not analytics, is the binding constraint on adopting honest measurement; leaders can pre-build credibility by first measuring a program expected to win.
insights.quote: This is the most common backlash following first-time incrementality testing.
insights.locator: Political Factors

insights.claim: Leaders compensated on quarterly metrics push to shrink holdouts and exploration budgets, so CDAOs and CMOs must frame exploration as an investment in information whose compounding returns from faster learning overcome the short-term revenue cost within quarters.
insights.speaker: Silvio Palumbo
insights.so_what: The tension between short-term revenue and learning budget is a recurring governance failure mode; reframing exploration as compounding information capital is the proposed fix.
insights.quote: framing exploration as an investment in information, not a cost to revenue
insights.locator: Budget Tension

insights.claim: As agents compose thousands of unique action sequences from a shelf of hundreds of components, traditional A/B testing breaks down because sample sizes fragment, forcing experimental design itself to become autonomous via adaptive techniques like multi-armed bandits and causal learning.
insights.speaker: Silvio Palumbo
insights.so_what: Static test/control frameworks won't scale to agentic marketing, favoring adaptive-experimentation tooling and vendors that dynamically allocate exploration traffic.
insights.locator: From Human-Designed Experiments to Agent-Designed Experiments

insights.claim: Agentic measurement compresses the time from insight to action from weeks or days to hours by closing the loop so agents adjust shelf composition, targeting, and budget within governance boundaries rather than handing findings to a separate marketing team.
insights.speaker: Silvio Palumbo
insights.so_what: Eliminating the measurement-to-decision handoff latency is the core value proposition of agent-native marketing systems and a competitive differentiator to watch.
insights.quote: The time from insight to action compresses from weeks or days to hours.
insights.locator: From Insight to Action

insights.claim: The recommended minimum starting point is combining in-platform metrics with Big M global-holdout measurement, which requires organizational discipline and cross-BU coordination rather than new technology or investment.
insights.speaker: Silvio Palumbo
insights.so_what: The first credible step toward measurement maturity is cheap in capital but expensive in coordination, so execution risk lies in governance, not budget.
insights.quote: This approach does not entail new technology or investment, but it does require organizational discipline and internal coordination
insights.locator: The minimum starting point for this inquiry
